title: program annual update--Library--Fall 2018 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
Program: Library Date: December 5, 2018
Faculty Completing Form: Jude Baldwin
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
The Library’s move to single sign-on in January 2018 was successful. This appears to have simplified off-campus access
since we no longer receive a steady stream of inquiries from students regarding specific difficulties or the process in
general. While single sign-on has been a benefit to our students (and employees) it has also had a minor downside: we
have lost a point of in-person student contact that caused many students to make a trip into the library. The Library’s
gate count went down from an average of 133 people per day in Fall 2017 to 101 people per day in Spring 2018.
Noticing this trend, the Library is trying several new tactics to encourage students to use the library more often. The
Library started a marketing campaign this fall with slides in the EagleVision slideshow in the Student Center and LRC
Lobby promoting many of our core services: research assistance and materials, reserve textbooks, computers, evening
hours, and both quiet and group study areas. Reformatted images have also been submitted to the Publicity Office to be
posted weekly on the college’s social media platform. The Library also created bookmarks that were put in every Lodge
student mailbox promoting Library and ASC services during mid-terms.
The Library is in the process of updating our space to make it more appealing to students. In Spring 2018 we replaced
our computer chairs, purchased two new armchairs, and two seating stools for the new book area. We hope to gradually
replace outdated furniture with more student-friendly options. Existing armchairs had their upholstery cleaned by a
local cleaning company over Spring Break 2018 and the facelift has them looking less shabby.
As part of the redesign, we also rebranded the “loft” area as the Study Lounge, moving in a few additional comfortable
armchairs and tables and allowing food for the first time. The Library promoted the Study Lounge in our general
marketing campaign and with a Grand Opening (coffee/tea all day and cookies in the afternoon). Future enhancements
to the Lounge include a projection screen and sound system so that the Library can host more speakers, movies, and
other events. Sound muffling panels and updated easier-to-move furniture are also on our wish list. In addition to
collaborating with the Writers Club for our annual spring Poetry Night celebration, we hope to develop new partnerships
with the Lodges and other areas on campus for evening use of this space.
To provide additional statistics, the Library now has a sign-in kiosk using TutorTrac, the same sign-in system used by the
ASC. The kiosk was installed in late Spring 2018 so that the Library could get used to the new system. We are looking
forward to the additional use statistics that TutorTrac will provide to supplement our very general gate count numbers.
The kiosk has presented a couple of challenges that we are still working on: getting students to log out when leaving and
dealing with community members. Until students start remembering to log out, the “length of visit” data will not be very
useful. Community members are often surprised, and sometimes irritated, at being stopped as they enter the library and
asked about signing in. This is not our past practice and is quite different from entering a public library. Once someone
has been identified as a community member, staff manually logs their visit into TutorTrac. Because students must now
log in at the kiosk, the Library no longer requires them to sign out a computer. While students are enjoying this
convenience, it will cause a decrease in circulation statistics and reduces the accuracy of our statistics about student
computer use.
Starting with the Fall 2017 semester, the Library has stayed open until 7:00 p.m. on Mondays and Wednesdays. These
are our first evening hours in approximately eight years. While use has been light, students often express their

title: program annual update--Library--Fall 2018 (Part 3)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
appreciation for these evening hours. In general, most of the use is between 5:00 – 6:00. We had more favorable
responses on our Spring 2018 Annual Student Survey to the question “The library provides adequate access to its
services and collections. (Consider things like our hours, Internet access, and service to online and distance users)”: 91%
of students stated that they “strongly agreed” or “agreed” (only 2% disagreed). This number is up from previous years:
78% in 2017, 77% in 2016, and 83% in 2015. 2018 is also the first time many years that we did not get written comments
regarding evening hours. We do still get requests for weekend hours.
The Library was open just one day a week (Thursdays) during the college’s first Winter Session. Student use of the
Library was very light with an average of 45 people day (much of this number is from Maintenance employees working
on the service desk relocation project). It is unknown if our very reduced hours met student needs.
Hours during Summer 2018 were also reduced: instead of 6 hours a day, 4 days per week (our hours in 2017), the Library
was open from 8:00 – 5:00 (9 hours), 2 days per week (Monday and Wednesday). While the average number of people
served per day stayed steady, the total number of people served over the length of the semester went down by 473
(from 1183 in 2017 to 710). In summer 2016 we actually offered fewer hours than in 2018 (4 hours a day, 4 days per
week) but were visited by over 1200 people. We may better serve our users by being open more days during the week,
even if we have fewer hours per day. It is only fair to note that, like other campus libraries, our gate count numbers have
been slowly declining each year, including in the summer. Our summer gate count was also impacted by the significantly
reduced number of SOAR tours on campus.
total gate count total open days
total open
hours
average gate
count per day
Summer 2016 1231 33 132 37
Summer 2017 1183 34 204 35
Summer 2018 710 19 171 37
The Library made Reserve textbooks available for use in the ASC when we were closed on Tuesdays and Thursdays; the
ASC was open for 4 hours, 4 days per week during the summer semesters. Due to ASC staff shortages, Linda Kiely helped
on the ASC service desk during their open hours for the first six weeks of the semester.
After many years of effort, the Library expanded the Reserve Collection to Yreka Campus. Working with Yreka Campus
staff, the Library put together a collection that focuses on textbooks being used in Yreka-based classes and in online
classes that are popular with Yreka students. The collection in Fall 2018 is small and will grow over time.
The COS Library collaborated with the County Library to offer students and employees a way to register for public library
cards. This will benefit our students by expanding the access they have to materials, especially reading for pleasure. The
two libraries are currently working on a better system to send materials between COS and the various branch libraries so
that our collections are more available to all Siskiyou County residents. We continue to connect with our community as a
partner with Siskiyou Reads, a non-profit organization affiliated with the public library that encourages all county
residents to read and discuss a book in common.
The Library has partnered with the Music program to organize and re-house their instrumental sheet music. This will
make the sheet music a more usable collection and the Music program will not spend funds unnecessarily on sheet
music they already own. It is requiring a large time investment from library staff and student employees. As of October
31, 2018 two of the four cabinets of instrumental music in the band room have been completed—1113 music titles have
been organized and rehoused. The focus of the project thus far has been on the band room cabinets which were

title: program annual update--Library--Fall 2018 (Part 5)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
deemed most critical but more sheet music is stored in other areas of the Performing Arts Building; this is a multi-year
project. Music faculty are already experiencing the benefits of the project: “Having the music all organized in the right
shelves and labeled in a way I can quickly access has made choosing literature much faster and easier. I can now even
quickly pull a chart during rehearsal to sight read.” (David Blink)
The statewide contract for a suite of EBSCO article databases for all community colleges was renegotiated in Fall 2017
and resulted in some changes. The Library received some cost savings when the statewide contract added the upgrade
to Academic Search, something which we had been paying for ourselves. Two databases were cut from the new
contract, Professional Development Collection and Religion and Philosophy Collection; after consulting with discipline
faculty only Professional Development Collection was continued (now out of the Library’s own budget). The Library took
advantage of the cost savings to add a database often requested by faculty: JSTOR. Dr. Zweigle consulted faculty in
various disciplines and it was decided that the JSTOR Arts & Sciences I collection would best suit campus needs. JSTOR
was added to the Library’s web site in July 2018.
The Library web site also added a new page with English as a Second Language resources in Siskiyou County. With
assistance from the Web Team, the Library has also redesigned all web site materials to meet accessibility standards.
After five years of effort, another statewide effort has taken off: funding was approved to move forward with the
selection of a vendor to provide a Library Services Platform (LSP) to all interested community college libraries. The
selection process took place in the spring and the LSP selected was Alma/Primo from Ex Libris, a division of ProQuest.
After careful consideration, the Library recommended that COS participate in the migration to the new LSP. While we
are satisfied with our current LSP, Worldshare Management Services (WMS) from OCLC, there are many benefits to
migrating:
• 107 of the 114 community college libraries (94%) are migrating therefore our students will learn transferable
skills and our students taking classes from multiple campuses will have familiar research experience
• The entire CSU system migrated to Alma/Primo, so our transferring students will have an easier transition with
their research assignments (SOU, OIT, the OSU system, and many UC’s also use Alma/Primo)
• Primo is reported to be a better discovery service that can connect researchers with materials in all of our
collections, including article databases (a weakness in our current LSP)
• Primo provides a more user-friendly and customizable interface
• Alma provides an increased opportunity for library partnerships and sharing within the California community
college system
By joining in the LSP Project, the college will benefit from reduced prices as well, especially if efforts to receive funding
to cover the first five years of subscription costs for all libraries is successful. Presentations about the LSP were made at
Academic Senate and Instruction Council to share information and gather feedback and concerns. Similar presentations
were also made at IPB and College Council in order to gain approval for the additional staffing needs required for the
migration; both approved moving forward with the migration. The Director of Technology Services has also been kept in
the loop about this project and the impact it will have on his department.
Last fall the Library wrote a Collection Development Policy which explains how materials are selected for and de-
selected from our collection. At the same time, the college decided to review all Board Policies and Administrative
Procedures and the Library was asked if we wanted to develop an AP4040 since none currently existed. It made sense to
incorporate the Collection Development Policy into AP4040 along with other Library policies. A first draft was reviewed

title: program annual update--Library--Fall 2018 (Part 6)
text:
-- 3 of 6 --

College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
by Instruction Council in May 2018 and is still receiving minor revisions; it will hopefully go to the Board by the end of
Fall 2018.
The Library’s Mount Shasta Collection continues to receive steady use. In March, we received a collection of
photographs, books, and calendars from former Siskiyou County resident, author, and professional photographer Jane
English for the Mt. Shasta Collection. The Library continues to provide materials and research support to the Sisson
Museum in Mount Shasta for their annual exhibits. Several academic works published this year were researched in part
using the Mt. Shasta Collection including:
• “The Giving River: How Organizational Actorhood and History Have Shaped the Management of Water,” by Brett
Crawford, Siddharth Mehra, and Yulong Hu in Managing Natural Resources: Organizational Strategy, Behavior
and Dynamics (Edward Elgar Pub., 2018)
• “Crystal Geyser, Water Activists and Mt. Shasta’s Eco-Esotericism” and “Ritualized Garments and Color-
Vibrational Esotericism in Angel Pageantry” by Dr. Madeline Duntley (conference presentations, American
Academy of Religion conference, November 2018)
• C. G. Pringle: Botanist, Traveler, and the “Flora of the Pacific Slope” (1881-1884), by Kathryn Mauz (NYGB Press,
2018)
The Library is scheduled for Comprehensive Program Review in AY2019-20. We plan on preparing for this detailed
assessment throughout AY2018-19 and are considering conducting a student focus group with the help of Nathan
Rexford in the Research Office to better understand student needs and expectations from the Library. We view the
Comprehensive Program Review process as an opportunity to develop a 5-year plan for the Library as well as update our
SLOs and PLOs.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
The Library uses the most recent Institutional Set Standards as agreed on by the Academic Senate when determining if
our SLOs have been met:
• Transfer courses (such as ENGL 1001): ≥67%
• Credit courses (such as ENGL 0900): ≥67%
• Basic Skills courses (such as ENGL 0850): ≥45%
• Vocational courses: ≥70%
Library SLO #1: Students who receive formal research instruction provided by the COS Library will be able to effectively
locate needed information using a variety of library resources.
This SLO is evaluated primarily by post-session evaluations; in-class worksheets and activities are also used when
possible. Evaluation questions tied to this SLO ask students to rate themselves on the following statements:
• The Library visit helped me to feel confident that I can get to the Library web site and databases page
• The Library visit helped me to feel comfortable finding articles in EBSCO (e.g. Academic Search, MasterFILE, etc.)
• The Library visit helped me to feel confident choosing keywords for a topic
Students consistently report that they are confident with these topics at the end of the session. For example, after
Librarian visits to Liz Carlyle’s three ENGL 1001 classes in Spring 2018:
• 94% felt confident about getting to the Library web site and databases page

-- 4 of 6 --

title: program annual update--Library--Fall 2018 (Part 7)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
• 100% felt comfortable finding articles in EBSCO (e.g. Academic Search, MasterFILE, etc.)
• 88% felt confident choosing keywords for a topic
On the Library’s 2018 annual Student Survey, 83% of respondents reported that “The library provides information and
instruction for students to help them develop their research skills. (Consider things like staff assistance, information
handouts, the library web site & classroom instruction.)” (rated as Strongly Agree or Agree on survey).
Library SLO #2: Students who participate in formal research instruction will be able to evaluate for credibility information
provided by the library, Internet resources, and other information sources.
This was not a topic the Librarian was asked to cover in class visits therefore the Library did not have the opportunity to
support or assess this SLO in formal instruction sessions.
Library SLO #3: Students who use the COS Library and utilize its resources, services, and formal instruction will appreciate
the value of libraries in supporting academic and/or career success and in supporting life-long learning.
On the Library’s 2018 annual Student Survey, 97% percent of respondents reported that “Having used the COS Library’s
resources, services, and instruction, I appreciate the value of libraries in supporting student success” (rated as Strongly
Agree or Agree on survey).
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
Since the retirement of Nancy Shepard in November 2016 the Library no longer reports to an administrator with
experience working in or managing a library. The Dean of Instruction has many competing claims on his time and
learning about management of such a different area has been challenging. Day-to-day management of the library often
falls to the Faculty Librarian in addition to her faculty responsibilities.
With a staff of just three people, it has sometimes been a challenge to be open our additional evening hours.
Library hours for the Winter and Summer sessions were limited due to lack of support and/or funding for adjunct
librarian contracts. Having just one librarian on staff is a limiting factor for library hours since Ed Code implies that a
librarian should be available during all open hours. If the College ever does a survey or other student assessment of
Winter and Summer sessions, we would very much like to see a question about student satisfaction regarding Library
hours.
The Library would like to update its facility to be a more appealing space for students. With tight budgets on campus,
the Library anticipates doing this redesign gradually over several years. Finding funding will be a challenge.
The integration between Banner and the Library’s WMS and EZProxy systems had an unexpected failure in the middle of
Spring 2018. The Banner—WMS integration manages the transfer of student and employee information into the library’s
circulation system. The Banner—EZProxy integration limits off-campus authentication to only current users (i.e. students
and employees). Technology Services re-programmed the Banner—WMS integration but did not have available staff to
fix the Banner—EZProxy integration to limit off-campus authentication to current users. While this lack of integration
does not impact student or employee off-campus access to our online materials, it does put us out of compliance with
several vendor contracts. These contracts stipulate that access is limited to current users only and as long as the
Banner—EZProxy integration remains unfixed we will be out of compliance with this requirement of our vendor
contracts. This needs to be fixed when Technical Services staff is available.
4. Describe program budgetary needs or implications. (Attach completed CQIP form.)

title: program annual update--Library--Fall 2018 (Part 8)
text:
-- 5 of 6 --

College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
The LSP migration will require that library staff spend a significant amount of time on the project from January 2019
through January 2020. This additional work will necessitate additional hours for the Faculty Librarian in Summer 2019,
December 2019, and January 2020 and/or out-of-class pay for classified staff members. These staffing needs were made
clear at presentations to Instruction Council, IPB, and College Council, all of which approved the move forward with the
migration. Parts of this project will need to be completed by Technology Services.
The Banner-EZProxy integration should be fixed so that we are back in compliance with our vendor contracts. This
project requires programmer expertise and therefore will have an impact on Technology Services.
We anticipate working with the Music program on their sheet music project at least through AY19-20. We have enough
supplies but advocate for continued funding for a student employee dedicated to this project. This position is funded
through the Music Department.
If the Faculty Librarian is unable to work an evening shift unexpectedly (5:00 – 7:00 p.m.), the Library needs to have a
backup plan to cover these hours. The Library needs guidance on whether or not classified overtime pay or
compensatory time is an option. If not (or if classified staff exercise their right to turn down compensatory time), the
Library will need to close early. This may have a negative impact on students who have planned on using Library
materials and/or services in the evening.
The Library would like to update its facility to be a more appealing space for students. With tight budgets on campus,
the Library is approaching this as a gradual redesign over several years, thoughtfully blending the old and the new. With
the help of Research, the Library plans to conduct a student focus group in the Spring to identify and incorporate
student needs, tastes, and expectations into our planning. By the end of the Spring semester, the Library would like to
have an overall vision of what our space will include after the redesign. Furniture is expensive and we anticipate making
similar requests for several years.
The Library would like the Study Lounge space to be able to accommodate presentations, movie nights, and other
events. We will need a projection screen, sound system, and media cart to transport the equipment. Hopefully our
existing laptop and projector will be adequate. We would also like to replace some furniture with pieces that are easier
to move and reconfigure for events.
The Faculty Librarian currently has a 195-day contract, which includes only 20 days to be applied towards Winter and
Summer hours. In order to offer more robust hours in Winter and Summer, the Library budget needs to contain funding
for additional days beyond the existing contract.
Having an adjunct librarian would be helpful. The Library would be able to better manage existing hours, possibly even
increase hours, and ease the current workload of the Faculty Librarian.

-- 6 of 6 --
